[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/First_photo_posted_to_POTUS_Biden_Administration_IG.jpg/250px-First_photo_posted_to_POTUS_Biden_Administration_IG.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_photo_posted_to_POTUS_Biden_Administration_IG.jpg)

Biden giving a speech as President-elect.

The [presidential transition](/wiki/United_States_presidential_transition "United States presidential transition") of [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") began on November 7, 2020, and ended on January 20, 2021. Unlike previous presidential transitions, which normally take place during the roughly 10-week period between the election in the first week of November and the inauguration on January 20, Biden's presidential transition was shortened because the [General Services Administration](/wiki/General_Services_Administration "General Services Administration") under the outgoing [first Trump administration](/wiki/First_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "First presidency of Donald Trump") did not recognize Biden as the "apparent winner" until November 23.

Biden became the presumptive nominee of the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") for [president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") in April 2020, and formally accepted the nomination the following August. Biden's transition team, led by [Ted Kaufman](/wiki/Ted_Kaufman "Ted Kaufman"), had already been announced on June 20. Further co-chairs joined the team alongside Kaufman in September. The [2020 presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election") took place on November 3. That evening, incumbent president [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") declared himself the winner, based on his initial lead in tabulated in-person votes — a situation which was widely anticipated and quickly discredited as meaningless, since the votes counted at the time were not representative of the final total and it takes several days to count all the votes. Trump [continued to falsely insist that he had won](/wiki/Attempts_to_overturn_the_2020_United_States_presidential_election "Attempts to overturn the 2020 United States presidential election"), alleging without evidence that Biden's increasing lead was due to widespread fraud, corruption, and other misconduct. He challenged the results in multiple lawsuits in multiple states, none of which resulted in a substantive victory.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Because of Trump's denials, there was a several-week delay before his administration began even limited cooperation with the Biden team.

After three and a half days of vote counting, on November 7, at approximately 11:30 a.m. EST, the [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press"), along with major TV networks including [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN"), [ABC News](/wiki/ABC_News_(United_States) "ABC News (United States)"), [CBS News](/wiki/CBS_News "CBS News"), [NBC News](/wiki/NBC_News "NBC News"), and [Fox News](/wiki/Fox_News "Fox News"), called the race for Joe Biden. After that, most sources described him as the president-elect. Nonetheless, [GSA administrator](/wiki/GSA_administrator "GSA administrator") [Emily Murphy](/wiki/Emily_W._Murphy "Emily W. Murphy"), a Trump appointee, waited until November 23 to issue the "ascertainment" letter declaring Biden the "apparent winner"[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) on the basis that Trump still disputed the election result. The declaration marked the official start of the transition, and withholding it from the Biden team had denied them $6.3 million, office space, [government website status](/wiki/.gov ".gov"), and access to agencies.[[7]](#cite_note-washingtonpost1-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Separately, Biden was denied [daily classified national security briefings](/wiki/President%27s_Daily_Brief "President's Daily Brief")[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) until the Trump administration approved Biden's receiving such briefings on November 24.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The [Electoral College](/wiki/Electoral_College_(United_States) "Electoral College (United States)") met on December 14, 2020, to formally elect Biden and [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris") respectively, as [president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") and [vice president](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_United_States "Vice President of the United States"). The results were to be [certified](/wiki/2021_United_States_Electoral_College_vote_count "2021 United States Electoral College vote count") by a [joint session of Congress](/wiki/Joint_session_of_the_United_States_Congress "Joint session of the United States Congress") on January 6, 2021, but due to an attempt by Trump supporters to overturn the results by [storming and vandalizing the Capitol building](/wiki/January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack "January 6 United States Capitol attack"), the certification was not completed until January 7.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) Biden's transition ended when he was [inaugurated](/wiki/Inauguration_of_Joe_Biden "Inauguration of Joe Biden") on January 20, 2021, at which point [his presidency](/wiki/Presidency_of_Joe_Biden "Presidency of Joe Biden") began.

## Background issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidential_transition_of_Joe_Biden&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background issues")]

Main article: [Attempts to overturn the 2020 United States presidential election § Refusal to accept 2020 electoral loss](/wiki/Attempts_to_overturn_the_2020_United_States_presidential_election#Refusal_to_accept_2020_electoral_loss "Attempts to overturn the 2020 United States presidential election")

### Preparations for potential disputed election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidential_transition_of_Joe_Biden&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Preparations for potential disputed election")]

See also: [Big lie § Donald Trump's false claims of a stolen election](/wiki/Big_lie#Donald_Trump's_false_claims_of_a_stolen_election "Big lie"), and [Post-election lawsuits related to the 2020 United States presidential election](/wiki/Post-election_lawsuits_related_to_the_2020_United_States_presidential_election "Post-election lawsuits related to the 2020 United States presidential election")

As early as the summer of 2020, President Donald Trump began questioning the legitimacy of the 2020 election, saying that the increase of [mail-in voting in the election](/wiki/Postal_voting_in_the_2020_United_States_elections "Postal voting in the 2020 United States elections") compared to previous elections will lead to a "rigged election".[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) For this reason, many pundits and editorial writers would insist that Biden would need to win by a [landslide](/wiki/Landslide_victory "Landslide victory") to prevent Trump from challenging the result.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-politico-20) Trump's preemptive accusations of fraud led some people to consider what would happen if he should lose by a margin less than a landslide.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) At various points, Trump had called for his [attorney general](/wiki/United_States_Attorney_General "United States Attorney General"), [William Barr](/wiki/William_Barr "William Barr"), to investigate Biden and his son [Hunter](/wiki/Hunter_Biden "Hunter Biden"), with Trump frequently insisting that his opponent should be in prison.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

[Rosa Brooks](/wiki/Rosa_Brooks "Rosa Brooks"), who worked in the [Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense") during the [Obama administration](/wiki/Obama_administration "Obama administration"), co-founded the [Transition Integrity Project](/wiki/Transition_Integrity_Project "Transition Integrity Project") (TIP), which in June 2020 ran a series of "wargaming" exercises to explore potential election and transition scenarios. In August 2020, TIP released a widely discussed [report](https://www.msn.com/en-us/news/politics/read-the-transition-integrity-projects-full-report/ar-BB17x0w3) that outlined four 2020 election crisis scenario planning exercises for the [2020 United States presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election").[[24]](#cite_note-24) The scenarios examined by TIP included a decisive Biden win, a decisive Trump win, a narrow Biden win, and a period of extended uncertainty after the election.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Other academics, such as Lawrence Douglas in his book *Will He Go?: Trump and the Looming Election Meltdown in 2020*, have also discussed the possibility, which later turned out to be true, of Trump refusing to concede if he lost.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Per the [20th Amendment](/wiki/Twentieth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twentieth Amendment to the United States Constitution"), the vice president must count the electoral votes in front of Congress's joint session on January 6, 2021. This is governed by the [Electoral Count Act of 1887](/wiki/Electoral_Count_Act_of_1887 "Electoral Count Act of 1887"), passed to prevent crises such as that in [1876–77](/wiki/Compromise_of_1877 "Compromise of 1877"), and while provisions have been used, the act has never been truly put to the test.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The Biden legal team had prepared for this, drafting basic responses to each of the possible litigations gamed out by the TIP and others.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The only one of the more than sixty cases which had not been dismissed out of hand was about late-arriving mail-in ballots.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Delays in initiating the transition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidential_transition_of_Joe_Biden&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Delays in initiating the transition")]

Main article: [Emily W. Murphy § 2020 presidential transition](/wiki/Emily_W._Murphy#2020_presidential_transition "Emily W. Murphy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Emily_W._Murphy_official_photo.jpg/250px-Emily_W._Murphy_official_photo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emily_W._Murphy_official_photo.jpg)

[Administrator of General Services](/wiki/General_Services_Administration "General Services Administration") [Emily Murphy](/wiki/Emily_W._Murphy "Emily W. Murphy") withheld transition resources until November 23.

Though Joe Biden was generally acknowledged as [president-elect](/wiki/President-elect_of_the_United_States "President-elect of the United States") in the election on November 7, 2020,[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) [General Services Administration](/wiki/General_Services_Administration "General Services Administration") head [Emily Murphy](/wiki/Emily_W._Murphy "Emily W. Murphy") refused to initiate the transition to the president-elect, thereby denying funds and office space to his team.[[7]](#cite_note-washingtonpost1-7)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Murphy refused to sign a letter (the "ascertainment" determination) allowing Biden's transition team to formally begin work to facilitate an [orderly transition of power](/wiki/Orderly_transition_of_power "Orderly transition of power"). By refusing to allow the Biden administration transition to proceed, she prevented the incoming administration from obtaining office space, performing background checks on prospective Cabinet nominees, and accessing classified information which might be needed to respond to emergencies that the administration confronts when in office. Murphy's withholding of the letter also blocked President-elect Biden's transition team from accessing several million dollars in federal transition funds for salaries and other costs, establishing government email addresses, and working with the [Office of Government Ethics](/wiki/Office_of_Government_Ethics "Office of Government Ethics") on required financial disclosure and conflict-of-interest forms for incoming nominees.
Amid public speculation that her refusal might jeopardize national security and public health, Murphy began a job search for herself, inquiring about employment opportunities in 2021.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

On November 8, the nonpartisan Center for Presidential Transition issued a statement saying "We urge the Trump administration to immediately begin the post-election transition process and the Biden team to take full advantage of the resources available under the [Presidential Transition Act](/wiki/Presidential_Transition_Act_of_1963 "Presidential Transition Act of 1963")."[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-:2-39) The letter was signed by several experts in presidential transitions: [Joshua Bolten](/wiki/Joshua_Bolten "Joshua Bolten"), President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush")'s former chief of staff; [Mike Leavitt](/wiki/Mike_Leavitt "Mike Leavitt"), former governor of Utah and Bush's Secretary of Health and Human Services; [Mack McLarty](/wiki/Mack_McLarty "Mack McLarty"), Bill Clinton's former chief of staff; and [Penny Pritzker](/wiki/Penny_Pritzker "Penny Pritzker"), Barack Obama's Secretary of Commerce.[[39]](#cite_note-:2-39)

[Andrew Card](/wiki/Andrew_Card "Andrew Card"), the first White House chief of staff under George W. Bush, expressed concern about the delay, noting that "the 9/11 Commission had said if there had been a longer transition [in 2000] and there had been cooperation, there might have been a better response, or maybe not even any attack".[[40]](#cite_note-40) Four former Secretaries of Homeland Security, [Tom Ridge](/wiki/Tom_Ridge "Tom Ridge"), [Michael Chertoff](/wiki/Michael_Chertoff "Michael Chertoff"), [Janet Napolitano](/wiki/Janet_Napolitano "Janet Napolitano"), and [Jeh Johnson](/wiki/Jeh_Johnson "Jeh Johnson"), called upon Murphy to initiate the transition, writing in a joint statement: "Our country is in the middle of twin crises: a global pandemic and a severe economic downturn. The pandemic will make any transition more complicated. At this period of heightened risk for our nation, we do not have a single day to spare to begin the transition. For the good of the nation, we must start now."[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[Anthony Fauci](/wiki/Anthony_Fauci "Anthony Fauci"), the government's leading infectious disease expert, warned the delay was "obviously not good" from a public health perspective, while President-elect Biden argued "more people may die" as a result of the delay.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

On November 23, after Michigan certified its results, Murphy issued the letter of ascertainment, granting the Biden transition team access to federal funds and resources for an orderly transition.[[45]](#cite_note-JALONICK-45) Breaking with recent precedent, the letter did not call Biden "president-elect", instead fulfilling her requirements under the Act without implying that he won the election.[[46]](#cite_note-:5-46) In the letter Murphy called the Act "vague", recommended Congress "consider amendments to the Act" to improve the standard it sets for post-election allocation of resources, and described threats she had allegedly received pressuring her to act.[[47]](#cite_note-:4-47)

## Timeline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidential_transition_of_Joe_Biden&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Timeline")]

### Pre-election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidential_transition_of_Joe_Biden&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Pre-election")]

Meetings between the transition team and the administration began with the formation of two councils in May 2020,[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-auto-49) around the time the former vice president had clinched the Democratic nomination.

* April 8, 2020: Biden becomes the presumptive nominee after [Bernie Sanders](/wiki/Bernie_Sanders "Bernie Sanders") withdraws.[[50]](#cite_note-50)
* June 20, 2020: Initial transition team announced.[[51]](#cite_note-cnn-51)
* August 2020: Biden and California Senator [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris") are nominated at the Democratic Convention.
* September 5, 2020: Full transition team is made public.[[51]](#cite_note-cnn-51)
* November 1, 2020: Deadline for transition materials to be completed.[[52]](#cite_note-52)
* November 3, 2020: Election Day

### Post-election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidential_transition_of_Joe_Biden&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Post-election")]

* November 4: The transition website, buildbackbetter.com, goes live.[[53]](#cite_note-53)
* November 7: Election is called for Biden.[[54]](#cite_note-54)
* November 8: GSA Administrator Emily Murphy refuses to sign a letter declaring the official start of the transition, denying funds and office space to the Biden team.[[7]](#cite_note-washingtonpost1-7)
* November 10: Transition Co-chair [Ted Kaufman](/wiki/Ted_Kaufman "Ted Kaufman") announces full transition team of at least 500 people.[[55]](#cite_note-55)
* November 23: Emily Murphy signs the transition letter.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The transition website changed its web address from buildbackbetter.com to buildbackbetter.gov
* December 9: Election results in every state and D.C. are certified.
* December 11: Supreme Court rejects *[Texas v. Pennsylvania](/wiki/Texas_v._Pennsylvania "Texas v. Pennsylvania")* challenge, mooting all others.
* December 14: The Electoral College formally elects Biden as president and Harris as vice president.
* December 18: The Acting Secretary of Defense surprises the Biden transition team with a "mutually agreed to" suspension of contacts until January.
* January 6, 2021: Congress [counts Electoral College votes](/wiki/2021_United_States_Electoral_College_vote_count "2021 United States Electoral College vote count"); interrupted by the [January 6 United States Capitol attack](/wiki/January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack "January 6 United States Capitol attack").
* January 7, 2021: Congress completes [counting Electoral College votes](/wiki/2021_United_States_Electoral_College_vote_count "2021 United States Electoral College vote count") and officially confirms the election results.
* January 13, 2021: President Trump is [impeached](/wiki/Second_impeachment_of_Donald_Trump "Second impeachment of Donald Trump") for the second time.
* January 20, 2021: [Inauguration Day](/wiki/Inauguration_of_Joe_Biden "Inauguration of Joe Biden")

## Transition procedures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidential_transition_of_Joe_Biden&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Transition procedures")]

Main article: [United States presidential transition](/wiki/United_States_presidential_transition "United States presidential transition")

In accordance with the [Pre-Election Presidential Transition Act of 2010](/wiki/Pre-Election_Presidential_Transition_Act_of_2010 "Pre-Election Presidential Transition Act of 2010"), potential presidential transition teams are provided office space by the [General Services Administration](/wiki/General_Services_Administration "General Services Administration") (GSA). They are also eligible for government funding for staff; spending on Mitt Romney's transition team in 2012 was $8.9 million, all funds appropriated by the [U.S. government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"). In order for the Biden transition team to formally begin working with federal agencies, access federal transition funds, and utilize government-provided office space, Biden had to be formally certified as president-elect by the administrator of the General Services Administration. After the media projected Biden to win the election, and as Trump refused to concede, the current GSA administrator, Trump appointee Emily W. Murphy, refused to sign a letter of authorization. She argued that she had seen no certification outside of the media that Biden has won, and was under no obligation to treat Biden as president-elect.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Key responsibilities of a presidential transition include the identification and vetting of candidates for approximately 4,000 non-civil service positions in the U.S. government who serve at the pleasure of the president; arranging the occupancy of executive residences including the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"), [One Observatory Circle](/wiki/One_Observatory_Circle "One Observatory Circle"), and [Camp David](/wiki/Camp_David "Camp David"); liaising with the [United States Strategic Command](/wiki/United_States_Strategic_Command "United States Strategic Command") for receipt of the [Gold Codes](/wiki/Gold_Codes "Gold Codes"); and briefing [senior personnel](/wiki/Civil_service "Civil service") about a new administration's policy priorities.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

Under existing federal law and custom, Biden became eligible to receive classified national security briefings when his nomination was formalized at the [party's national convention](/wiki/2020_Democratic_National_Convention "2020 Democratic National Convention") in August 2020.

The Presidential Transition Act was amended in 2019 to require the incumbent president to establish "transition councils" by June of an election year to facilitate a possible handover of power.[[49]](#cite_note-auto-49)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

## Transition activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidential_transition_of_Joe_Biden&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Transition activities")]

### Establishment of the transition team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidential_transition_of_Joe_Biden&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Establishment of the transition team")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Biden_Transition_%2850727804701%29.jpg/250px-Biden_Transition_%2850727804701%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biden_Transition_(50727804701).jpg)

[Governor of Maryland](/wiki/Governor_of_Maryland "Governor of Maryland") [Larry Hogan](/wiki/Larry_Hogan "Larry Hogan") participates in a virtual meeting with Biden, Harris, and other governors on December 16

Biden began to appoint transition staff in May 2020, chaired by [Ted Kaufman](/wiki/Ted_Kaufman "Ted Kaufman"). Kaufman was previously appointed to Biden's senate seat following his resignation to become the Vice President.[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62) Further staff were announced in September. The campaign had an estimated budget of $7–10 million and planned to have a staff of 300 people by early December 2020. Several working groups were set up in late September and early October.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States"), much of the work was done over [Zoom](/wiki/Zoom_(software) "Zoom (software)").[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Biden launched his transition website at approximately 6:30 p.m. EST on November 4, a day after Election Day and before the final election results were in.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### Harris Senate transition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidential_transition_of_Joe_Biden&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Harris Senate transition")]

Harris resigned her Senate seat on January 18, 2021, two days before assuming the vice presidency.[[66]](#cite_note-66) On January 19, 2021, California Governor [Gavin Newsom](/wiki/Gavin_Newsom "Gavin Newsom") announced that California Secretary of State [Alex Padilla](/wiki/Alex_Padilla "Alex Padilla") succeeded Harris for the remainder of her term.[[67]](#cite_note-:3-67)

Main article: [Post-election lawsuits related to the 2020 United States presidential election](/wiki/Post-election_lawsuits_related_to_the_2020_United_States_presidential_election "Post-election lawsuits related to the 2020 United States presidential election")

See also: [Attempts to overturn the 2020 United States presidential election](/wiki/Attempts_to_overturn_the_2020_United_States_presidential_election "Attempts to overturn the 2020 United States presidential election") and [Texas v. Pennsylvania](/wiki/Texas_v._Pennsylvania "Texas v. Pennsylvania")

While the transition was ongoing, multiple lawsuits were filed to contest election procedures and results.[[68]](#cite_note-68) Most of these lawsuits were unsuccessful and were quickly dismissed.

### COVID-19 Advisory Board

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidential_transition_of_Joe_Biden&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: COVID-19 Advisory Board")]

Main article: [COVID-19 Advisory Board](/wiki/COVID-19_Advisory_Board "COVID-19 Advisory Board")

Before naming any White House staff or cabinet appointments, Biden announced that he will appoint a COVID-19 task force, co-chaired by former Surgeon General [Vivek Murthy](/wiki/Vivek_Murthy "Vivek Murthy"), former Food and Drug Administration Commissioner [David Kessler](/wiki/David_A._Kessler "David A. Kessler") and Yale University epidemiologist Professor [Marcella Nunez-Smith](/wiki/Marcella_Nunez-Smith "Marcella Nunez-Smith"), to replace the previous [White House Coronavirus Task Force](/wiki/White_House_Coronavirus_Task_Force "White House Coronavirus Task Force").[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)

Biden pledged a larger federal government response to [the pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States") than Donald Trump, akin to President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt")'s [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") following the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"). This would include increased testing for the COVID virus, a steady supply of [personal protective equipment](/wiki/Personal_protective_equipment "Personal protective equipment"), distributing a vaccine and securing money from Congress for schools and hospitals under the aegis of a national "supply chain commander" who would coordinate the logistics of manufacturing and distributing protective gear and test kits. This would be distributed by a "Pandemic Testing Board", also similar to Roosevelt's [War Production Board](/wiki/War_Production_Board "War Production Board").

Biden also pledged to invoke the [Defense Production Act](/wiki/Defense_Production_Act "Defense Production Act") more aggressively than Trump in order to build up supplies, as well as the mobilization of up to 100,000 Americans for a "public health jobs corps" of [contact tracers](/wiki/Contact_tracing "Contact tracing") to help track and prevent outbreaks.[[71]](#cite_note-GoodStol-71) "Other members are Dr. [Luciana Borio](/wiki/Luciana_Borio "Luciana Borio"), [Rick Bright](/wiki/Rick_Bright "Rick Bright"), Dr. [Ezekiel Emanuel](/wiki/Ezekiel_Emanuel "Ezekiel Emanuel"), Dr. [Atul Gawande](/wiki/Atul_Gawande "Atul Gawande"), Dr. [Celine Gounder](/wiki/Celine_Gounder "Celine Gounder"), Dr. [Julie Morita](/wiki/Julie_Morita "Julie Morita"), [Michael Osterholm](/wiki/Michael_Osterholm "Michael Osterholm"), [Loyce Pace](/wiki/Loyce_Pace "Loyce Pace"), Dr. [Robert Rodriguez](/wiki/Robert_Rodriguez_(physician) "Robert Rodriguez (physician)") and Dr. [Eric Goosby](/wiki/Eric_Goosby "Eric Goosby")."[[72]](#cite_note-72) Anthony Fauci confirmed on November 25 that he expected to begin working with the Biden transition team soon.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

### Cabinet and White House appointments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidential_transition_of_Joe_Biden&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Cabinet and White House appointments")]

Main articles: [Cabinet of Joe Biden](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Joe_Biden "Cabinet of Joe Biden") and [Political appointments by Joe Biden](/wiki/Political_appointments_by_Joe_Biden "Political appointments by Joe Biden")

On November 11, Biden announced his choice of [Ron Klain](/wiki/Ron_Klain "Ron Klain"), formerly his chief of staff during his vice presidency, to serve as [White House chief of staff](/wiki/White_House_Chief_of_Staff "White House Chief of Staff").[[74]](#cite_note-74) On November 17, [Mike Donilon](/wiki/Mike_Donilon "Mike Donilon") was named [Senior Advisor to the President](/wiki/Senior_Advisor_to_the_President "Senior Advisor to the President"), [Jen O'Malley Dillon](/wiki/Jen_O%27Malley_Dillon "Jen O'Malley Dillon") was named [deputy chief of staff](/wiki/White_House_Deputy_Chief_of_Staff "White House Deputy Chief of Staff"), [Steve Ricchetti](/wiki/Steve_Ricchetti "Steve Ricchetti") was named [Counselor to the President](/wiki/Counselor_to_the_President "Counselor to the President"), [Cedric Richmond](/wiki/Cedric_Richmond "Cedric Richmond") was named [Senior Advisor to the President of the United States](/wiki/Senior_Advisor_to_the_President_of_the_United_States "Senior Advisor to the President of the United States") and director of the [White House Office of Public Engagement](/wiki/White_House_Office_of_Public_Engagement "White House Office of Public Engagement"), and [Dana Remus](/wiki/Dana_Remus "Dana Remus") was named [Counsel to the President](/wiki/Counsel_to_the_President "Counsel to the President").[[75]](#cite_note-75) [Annie Tomasini](/wiki/Annie_Tomasini "Annie Tomasini") was named the Oval Office Operations Director.[[76]](#cite_note-76) On November 20, 2020, Biden named Louisa Terrell director of the [Office of Legislative Affairs](/wiki/White_House_Office#Office_of_Legislative_Affairs "White House Office"), Carlos Elizondo [White House Social Secretary](/wiki/White_House_Social_Secretary "White House Social Secretary"), [Catherine M. Russell](/wiki/Catherine_M._Russell "Catherine M. Russell") director of the [White House Presidential Personnel Office](/wiki/White_House_Presidential_Personnel_Office "White House Presidential Personnel Office"), and Mala Adiga policy director to the First Lady.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

On November 22, 2020, Bloomberg News and the *New York Times* reported that Biden had selected [Tony Blinken](/wiki/Tony_Blinken "Tony Blinken") as his nominee for secretary of state.[[78]](#cite_note-longtimeaide-78)[[79]](#cite_note-blinken-sos-nyt-nov-2020-79) The next day, Biden announced that he would nominate [Alejandro Mayorkas](/wiki/Alejandro_Mayorkas "Alejandro Mayorkas") to be [Secretary of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Homeland_Security "United States Secretary of Homeland Security"), [Avril Haines](/wiki/Avril_Haines "Avril Haines") to be the [Director of National Intelligence](/wiki/Director_of_National_Intelligence "Director of National Intelligence"), and [Linda Thomas-Greenfield](/wiki/Linda_Thomas-Greenfield "Linda Thomas-Greenfield") as [United States Ambassador to the United Nations](/wiki/United_States_Ambassador_to_the_United_Nations "United States Ambassador to the United Nations"), [Jake Sullivan](/wiki/Jake_Sullivan "Jake Sullivan") to be [National Security Advisor](/wiki/United_States_National_Security_Advisor "United States National Security Advisor"), and [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry") as special presidential envoy on climate.[[80]](#cite_note-80) It was also reported that Biden will nominate former [Chair of the Federal Reserve](/wiki/Chair_of_the_Federal_Reserve "Chair of the Federal Reserve") [Janet Yellen](/wiki/Janet_Yellen "Janet Yellen") to be [Treasury Secretary](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Secretary of the Treasury").[[81]](#cite_note-81)

### Department of Defense

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidential_transition_of_Joe_Biden&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Department of Defense")]

There was a great deal of resistance to Biden staffers by Trump appointees, and not much cooperation.[[112]](#cite_note-112) Then on December 18, acting Defense Secretary [Chris Miller](/wiki/Christopher_C._Miller "Christopher C. Miller") said in a statement that the transition team had agreed to a two-week pause in discussions over the December holiday period. This was news to the Biden transition team, who immediately said they had not agreed to any such thing.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

### Planned executive orders and guidelines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidential_transition_of_Joe_Biden&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Planned executive orders and guidelines")]

President-elect Joe Biden plans on signing several executive orders after being sworn into office on January 20, 2021. His planned executive orders include:

* Rejoining the [Paris Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement")
* Reversing President Trump's planned withdrawal from the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization")
* Reinstating the [Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals](/wiki/Deferred_Action_for_Childhood_Arrivals "Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals") program
* Repealing [Presidential Proclamation 9983](/wiki/Executive_Order_13780#Presidential_Proclamation_9983 "Executive Order 13780") and the [Presidential Memorandum on Military Service by Transgender Individuals](/wiki/Presidential_Memorandum_on_Military_Service_by_Transgender_Individuals_(2018) "Presidential Memorandum on Military Service by Transgender Individuals (2018)")
* Barring anyone in his administration from influencing any [United States Justice Department](/wiki/United_States_Justice_Department "United States Justice Department") investigations
* Instituting new ethics guidelines at the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House")[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[115]](#cite_note-115)
* Freezing so-called [midnight regulations](/wiki/Midnight_regulations "Midnight regulations") that have not taken place by January 20[[116]](#cite_note-116)

In addition to these executive orders, President-elect Biden plans to issue a large number of executive orders on day one relating to [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"),[[117]](#cite_note-117) and cancel several health care policies put in place by the Trump administration including [Medicaid](/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid") work requirements, short-term health plans, [Obamacare](/wiki/Obamacare "Obamacare") funding cuts, [Title X](/wiki/Title_X "Title X") abortion referral restrictions, and the [Mexico City Policy](/wiki/Mexico_City_Policy "Mexico City Policy"), as well as reinstating [Planned Parenthood](/wiki/Planned_Parenthood "Planned Parenthood") funding and restoring LGBTQ regulations.[[118]](#cite_note-118) President-elect Biden also stated he would urge all Americans to wear a mask for the first 100 days of his presidency in an effort to curb the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[119]](#cite_note-Dec9-119)
